prompts.name: odoo_getting_started
prompts.description: Orientation: how this MCP server maps to Odoo, which tool to pick, and how to target an instance
prompts.content:
# Odoo MCP — Getting Started

Use when: starting any task and you need to pick the right tool or target the right server.

## Tool Map (what to call for what)
- `odoo_search` -> ids only, for an existing domain. Cheapest discovery.
- `odoo_search_read` -> ids + selected fields in one call. The default for "find and show me".
- `odoo_read` -> fields for known `ids`.
- `odoo_count` -> number of matches for a domain (no records fetched).
- `odoo_read_group` -> aggregation / GROUP BY (totals, counts, time buckets). Never fetch-all-and-sum.
- `odoo_create` / `odoo_update` / `odoo_delete` -> write, write to existing, unlink. See `odoo_safety_and_writes` first.
- `odoo_workflow_action` -> confirm/post/cancel/validate state transitions (button actions).
- `odoo_execute` -> arbitrary model method when no dedicated tool fits.
- `odoo_get_model_metadata` -> field names, types, selection options before querying an unfamiliar model.
- `odoo_generate_report` -> render a QWeb report (PDF/HTML) for record ids.

## Multi-instance targeting
Every tool accepts an optional `instance` string. Omit it to use the default instance; set it to target a specific configured server, e.g. `{ "instance": "production", "model": "res.partner", ... }`.

## Version & auth (affects nothing in tool args, but explains behavior)
- Odoo 19+ : JSON-2 External API, API key auth.
- Odoo <19 : JSON-RPC, username/password auth.
The server picks the client from the instance's `version`/auth config — you call the same tools either way.

## A good first move on an unknown model
1. `odoo_get_model_metadata` to learn fields + selection values.
2. `odoo_count` to size the result.
3. `odoo_search_read` with an explicit `fields` list and a `limit`.

prompts.name: odoo_common_models
prompts.description: List of commonly used Odoo models
prompts.content:
# Common Odoo Models (v16-19)

Use when: you need the technical model name for a business concept.

## Sales & CRM
- sale.order — Sales Orders
- sale.order.line — Sales Order Lines
- crm.lead — Leads/Opportunities (type: lead | opportunity)
- crm.team — Sales Teams

## Accounting & Invoicing
- account.move — Invoices, Bills & Journal Entries (move_type: out_invoice, in_invoice, out_refund, in_refund, entry)
- account.move.line — Invoice/Bill/Entry Lines
- account.payment — Payments
- account.journal — Journals
- account.account — Chart of Accounts

## Inventory & Warehouse
- stock.picking — Transfers/Deliveries/Receipts
- stock.move — Stock Moves
- stock.move.line — Detailed Move Lines
- stock.warehouse / stock.location — Warehouses / Locations
- stock.quant — On-hand Quantities
- stock.lot — Serial/Lot Numbers

## Products
- product.product — Products (variants)
- product.template — Product Templates
- product.category — Categories
- uom.uom — Units of Measure
- product.pricelist — Pricelists

## Partners & Contacts
- res.partner — Contacts/Customers/Vendors
- res.company / res.users — Companies / Users
- res.country / res.currency — Countries / Currencies

## HR
- hr.employee / hr.department — Employees / Departments
- hr.leave / hr.contract — Time Off / Contracts

## Projects
- project.project / project.task / project.task.type — Projects / Tasks / Stages

## Purchase
- purchase.order / purchase.order.line — Purchase Orders / Lines

## POS
- pos.order / pos.order.line / pos.session / pos.config

prompts.name: odoo_domain_filters
prompts.description: Guide for Odoo domain filter syntax (the `domain` arg)
prompts.content:
# Odoo Domain Filters — the `domain` argument

Use when: building the `domain` for `odoo_search`, `odoo_search_read`, `odoo_count`, or `odoo_read_group`.

A domain is a list of triplets `[field, operator, value]` combined with prefix logical operators.

## Comparison Operators
- ['amount_total', '=', 1000] / '!=' / '>' / '>=' / '<' / '<='

## String / Pattern
- ['name', 'like', 'John']   contains, case-sensitive
- ['name', 'ilike', 'john']  contains, case-insensitive (most common)
- ['email', '=like', '%@example.com']  SQL LIKE pattern
- ['name', '=ilike', 'john%']           SQL ILIKE pattern

## Membership
- ['state', 'in', ['draft', 'posted']]
- ['state', 'not in', ['cancel']]
- ['partner_id', 'in', [3, 7, 12]]   (ids for Many2one)

## Logical Operators (prefix / Polish notation)
Default between triplets is AND. Operators apply to the next N items:
- '&' AND, '|' OR (binary — apply to next two), '!' NOT (unary — next one)

```
['&', ['state', '=', 'sale'], ['amount_total', '>', 1000]]
['|', ['state', '=', 'draft'], ['state', '=', 'sent']]
['!', ['state', '=', 'cancel']]
```

## Traverse related fields with dotted paths
- ['partner_id.country_id.code', '=', 'US']
- ['order_line.product_id.name', 'ilike', 'laptop']

## Dates (strings, server timezone for Datetime)
- ['invoice_date', '>=', '2024-01-01']
- ['create_date', '<=', '2024-12-31 23:59:59']

## Worked Example — confirmed US orders over $1000
```
{
  "model": "sale.order",
  "domain": [
    "&", ["state", "=", "sale"],
    "&", ["amount_total", ">", 1000],
         ["partner_id.country_id.code", "=", "US"]
  ],
  "fields": ["name", "partner_id", "amount_total"],
  "limit": 50
}
```

Tip: empty domain `[]` matches all records — always pair it with a `limit`.

prompts.name: odoo_field_types
prompts.description: Odoo field types and relational fields explained
prompts.content:
# Odoo Field Types

Use when: deciding what to put in `fields`/`values`, or interpreting results.

## Scalar Fields
- Char — short text (name, code)
- Text — long text (description, notes)
- Integer / Float — numbers
- Monetary — currency amount (needs a currency_id on the record)
- Boolean — true/false
- Date — 'YYYY-MM-DD'
- Datetime — 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS' (stored in UTC)
- Selection — fixed key set; read returns the KEY (e.g. 'sale'), not the label
- Binary — base64 file content
- Html — rich text

## Relational Fields
### Many2one (N:1)
Returns `[id, display_name]` on read, e.g. partner_id -> [42, "John Doe"].
Write by id: `"partner_id": 42`.

### One2many / Many2many
Read returns a list of ids. Write with command tuples:
- (0, 0, {values}) — create & link a new record
- (1, id, {values}) — update linked record
- (2, id) — delete linked record
- (3, id) — unlink (keep record)
- (4, id) — link existing
- (5,) — unlink all
- (6, 0, [ids]) — replace set with [ids]
Example: `"tag_ids": [[6, 0, [1, 2, 3]]]`, `"order_line": [[0, 0, {"product_id": 5, "product_uom_qty": 2}]]`

## Naming Conventions
- `_id` -> Many2one ; `_ids` -> One2many/Many2many ; `_count` -> computed count

Unsure of a model's fields? Call `odoo_get_model_metadata` first.

prompts.name: odoo_workflow_states
prompts.description: Common workflow states and how to transition them
prompts.content:
# Odoo Document Workflow States

Use when: filtering by `state` or driving transitions with `odoo_workflow_action`.

## sale.order — state
draft (Quotation) -> sent -> sale (confirmed) -> done (locked) | cancel
Actions: action_confirm, action_cancel, action_draft, action_done

## purchase.order — state
draft (RFQ) -> sent -> to approve -> purchase -> done | cancel
Actions: button_confirm, button_cancel, button_draft

## account.move
move_type: out_invoice | in_invoice | out_refund | in_refund | entry
state: draft -> posted | cancel  (actions: action_post, button_draft, button_cancel)
payment_state: not_paid | in_payment | partial | paid | reversed

## stock.picking — state
draft -> waiting -> confirmed -> assigned (Ready) -> done | cancel
picking_type_code: incoming | outgoing | internal
Actions: action_confirm, action_assign, button_validate, action_cancel

## crm.lead — type: lead | opportunity

## project.task — uses stage_id (Many2one project.task.type), not a state enum

## pos.order — state: draft -> paid -> done -> invoiced | cancel

Prefer `odoo_workflow_action` (model, ids, action) over `odoo_execute` for these button methods; it is the intended, guarded path.

prompts.name: odoo_read_group
prompts.description: How to use odoo_read_group for aggregation and reporting
prompts.content:
# Aggregation with `odoo_read_group`

Use when: you need totals, counts, averages, or time buckets. Pushes GROUP BY to the database — never fetch all rows and sum in the client.

## Arguments
- model, domain, fields (with :agg suffix), groupby, offset?, limit?, orderby?

## Aggregations: sum, count, avg, min, max
Apply as a suffix: `"amount_total:sum"`. `__count` is always returned.

## Time bucketing on date/datetime groupby
`field:day | week | month | quarter | year`, e.g. `"invoice_date:month"`.

## Examples
### Count orders by state
```
{ "model": "sale.order", "domain": [], "fields": ["__count"], "groupby": ["state"] }
```
### Confirmed sales total by customer
```
{
  "model": "sale.order",
  "domain": [["state", "=", "sale"]],
  "fields": ["amount_total:sum"],
  "groupby": ["partner_id"]
}
```
### Monthly posted revenue
```
{
  "model": "account.move",
  "domain": [["state", "=", "posted"], ["move_type", "=", "out_invoice"]],
  "fields": ["amount_total:sum"],
  "groupby": ["invoice_date:month"]
}
```

## Notes
- Returns a list of dicts, not records. Each row carries the groupby key(s) + aggregates + `__count`.
- Many2one groupby keys come back as `[id, name]`.
- Only stored fields can be grouped or aggregated (computed-non-stored cannot).

prompts.name: odoo_context
prompts.description: Odoo context parameters and how to pass them
prompts.content:
# Odoo `context`

Use when: you need to set defaults, switch company/lang/tz, or alter side effects. Every tool accepts an optional `context` object.

## Common Keys
### Session
- lang ('en_US', 'id_ID'), tz ('Asia/Jakarta', 'UTC'), uid, allowed_company_ids ([ids])
### Defaults & search
- default_<field> — default a value on create
- search_default_<filter> — preselect a search filter
### Side-effect modifiers (use deliberately)
- tracking_disable: true — skip mail tracking on write/create
- mail_create_nosubscribe: true / mail_auto_subscribe_no_notify: true — suppress follower noise

## Set defaults on create
```
{ "model": "sale.order", "values": { "partner_id": 42 },
  "context": { "default_pricelist_id": 1, "lang": "en_US" } }
```

## Force a company
```
{ "context": { "allowed_company_ids": [1] } }
```

## Pass context to a method via odoo_execute
For `odoo_execute`, business kwargs go in `kwargs`; `context` is its own top-level arg:
```
{ "model": "sale.order", "method": "action_confirm",
  "args": [[123]], "context": { "tracking_disable": true } }
```

Datetime values are UTC on the wire; `tz` only affects display/computed conversions, not the raw stored value.

prompts.name: odoo_api_tips
prompts.description: Best practices for querying through this MCP server
prompts.content:
# Odoo API Best Practices (via this MCP)

Use when: a query is slow, returns too much, or you want the cheapest correct call.

## Performance
1. Always pass an explicit `fields` list — never fetch every field.
2. Always pass `limit` (and `offset` to page). `limit` 50–200 is a sane default.
3. Use `odoo_count` to size before fetching; use `odoo_read_group` for any total/breakdown.
4. Filter in the `domain`, not in code. Use `in` to batch instead of one call per id.
5. Reach for `odoo_read` when you already hold the ids — skip the search.

## Reading results correctly
- Many2one -> `[id, name]`. Use `[0]` for the id, `[1]` for the label.
- Selection -> the stored KEY (e.g. 'posted'), not the human label. Use `odoo_get_model_metadata` for labels.
- Date/Datetime -> ISO strings; Datetime is UTC.

## Common shapes
### Existence check (cheap)
```
{ "model": "res.partner", "domain": [["email", "=", "a@b.com"]], "fields": ["id"], "limit": 1 }
```
### Record + related field
```
{ "model": "sale.order", "domain": [["id", "=", 123]],
  "fields": ["name", "state", "amount_total", "partner_id", "partner_id.email"] }
```
### One2many drill-down
Read the parent's line ids, then `odoo_search_read` the line model with `[["order_id", "=", 123]]`.

## Errors
Inspect the error message: access rights, missing record, or a Python ValidationError from a model constraint are the usual causes. Fix the domain/values rather than retrying blindly.

prompts.name: odoo_safety_and_writes
prompts.description: Guardrails for create/update/delete and the gated cleanup tools
prompts.content:
# Writes & Destructive Operations — Safety

Use when: a task would create, modify, delete, or bulk-clean data. Read this BEFORE the first write.

## Mindset
- Reads are free to explore; writes change a live ERP. Confirm scope with the user before mutating production data.
- Identify records first (`odoo_search`/`odoo_count`) and echo the count you are about to change. Never run an unbounded `odoo_update`/`odoo_delete`.

## Tools
- `odoo_create` — model + values. Returns the new id.
- `odoo_update` — model + ids + values. The ids list IS the blast radius; double-check it.
- `odoo_delete` — model + ids. Often blocked: posted/linked records usually cannot be unlinked. Prefer archiving (`{"active": false}` via `odoo_update`) when available.
- `odoo_workflow_action` — for state changes (confirm/post/cancel) instead of editing `state` directly.

## Cleanup tools are gated
`odoo_database_cleanup` and `odoo_deep_cleanup` are destructive and only run when the server is started with `ODOO_ENABLE_CLEANUP_TOOLS=true`. If disabled, they will refuse — do not work around the guard.
- ALWAYS run with `"dryRun": true` first and report what it would remove.
- Only set `"dryRun": false` after explicit user confirmation of the dry-run results.
- `odoo_deep_cleanup` is the most dangerous: prefer the `keep*` flags (keepCompanyDefaults, keepUserAccounts, keepMenus, keepGroups) and never run it against production without sign-off.

## Rule of thumb
If you cannot state exactly which records change and how many, you are not ready to write.

prompts.name: odoo_owl_components
prompts.description: Owl component structure and debugging patterns for Odoo addons
prompts.content:
# Odoo Owl Components

Use when: writing or debugging frontend JS components in a custom addon (not for data queries).

## Standard Addon Shape
- Colocate a triplet under static/src: JS (logic), XML (translatable template), SCSS (only if the component owns styling).
- First line of addon JS: `/** @odoo-module **/`.
- Template names globally unique: `addon_name.ComponentName`.

## Component Rules
- Use `setup()`, not a constructor.
- Keep production templates in XML, not inline JS strings.
- Small state objects, DOM-driven behavior.
- `useService()` only for genuinely long-lived app services.
- Reuse `@web` components before inventing primitives.

## First Debug Checks
- JS, XML, (SCSS) all listed in the correct asset bundle.
- `static template = "..."` matches the XML `t-name` exactly.
- Imports, relative paths, props, and parent mounting match the addon's existing structure.

prompts.name: odoo_assets_and_bundles
prompts.description: Asset bundle choices and module wiring for Odoo frontend code
prompts.content:
# Odoo Assets and Bundles

Use when: a custom addon's JS/XML/SCSS does not load or render.

## Common Bundles
- web.assets_backend — backend webclient code
- web.assets_frontend — portal/website code
- web.assets_unit_tests — frontend test assets
- Standalone pages need an explicit entrypoint in the correct runtime bundle.

## Manifest Rules
- Register JS, XML, and SCSS together in the manifest `assets` block.
- Keep `/** @odoo-module **/` on the first line of addon JS.
- `@web/...` imports for core, relative imports for addon-local files.

## Debug Checklist
- Does the page actually load the chosen bundle?
- Was the XML added alongside the JS?
- Is the module header on line one?
- Missing asset wiring is the first suspect when components don't render.

prompts.name: odoo_frontend_contexts
prompts.description: Choosing between backend, client action, standalone Owl, and website contexts
prompts.content:
# Odoo Frontend Contexts

Use when: deciding where new frontend code should live.

## Integration Models
- Existing backend screen — extend a current view/screen; reuse its env and services.
- Client action — a navigable backend screen rooted in the actions registry.
- Standalone Owl app — owns its own mount target and startup.
- Portal/website component — frontend website runtime, separate from the backend webclient.

## Practical Rules
- Stay close to the addon's existing structure when fixing/extending.
- For a brand-new backend app, choose explicitly between client action and standalone before coding.
- Re-evaluate services, registries, and asset bundles whenever the runtime context changes.

prompts.name: odoo_qweb_and_templates
prompts.description: QWeb and Owl template rules, directives, and version notes
prompts.content:
# Odoo QWeb and Owl Templates

Use when: writing report or component templates in XML.

## Core Rules
- Production components use XML templates (translatable, consistent loading).
- Keep `xml:space="preserve"` on the root `<templates>` block.
- Template name `addon_name.ComponentName` must match the JS `static template`/`t-name`.

## Directives
- t-if / t-elif / t-else — conditionals
- t-foreach with t-key — lists
- t-on-click and friends — events
- t-att-* / t-attf-* — dynamic attributes
- escaped output by default

## Version-Sensitive
- Odoo 18 templates commonly use `t-esc`.
- Odoo 19 increasingly prefers `t-out`.
- When editing around existing code, follow the nearby version style — no drive-by rewrites.

## Avoid
- Mismatched JS template reference vs XML `t-name`.
- Leaving translatable production templates inline in JS.
- Duplicate template names across addons.
